fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the spreadsheet export engine for the next release. Basically we want something that can take a bunch of in-memory spreadsheet data and spit out the right XML chunks that get assembled into a real .xlsx file. There are a bunch of little conversion helpers we need too — like turning grid coordinates into the letter-number cell names people are used to seeing, figuring out what type a value is when nobody tells us explicitly, and converting calendar dates into the weird number format spreadsheets use internally.

We also need serialization for all the layout-y stuff — margins, print settings, column widths, row heights, merged cells, filters, that kind of thing. Oh and protection settings with the password thing — similar to how we handled it in the auth module a while back, just a short hash, not storing it in plain text obviously.

The whole thing should be broken up into sensible files, not just one giant blob. Each operation should get called via a JSON command on stdin and print the result to stdout with no extra junk. Errors should come back as a clean normalized token, not raw exceptions.

Range references need to handle both relative and absolute forms, and the absolute ones need the sheet name quoted properly with special characters escaped. Date handling needs to support two different epoch modes. Basically just make sure all the edge cases are solid — invalid inputs, out-of-range numbers, unrecognized enum values, etc.

trigger_keywords: column letter conversion algorithm
how are column ordinals mapped to letters
bijective base-26 scheme details
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Column ordinals are zero-based and map using a bijective base-26 scheme: ordinal 0 → A, ordinal 25 → Z, ordinal 26 → AA, ordinal 702 → AAA, ordinal 727 → AAZ, ordinal 728 → ABA, ordinal 2048 → BZU. There is no 'zero' letter — after Z the labels grow to two letters (AA), not restart.

trigger_keywords: date epoch convention
which epoch should dates be counted from
1900 vs 1904 date serial number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two epoch conventions must be supported. The default (date1904=false) has day-zero predating 1900-01-01, so 1900-01-01 serializes to 2.0 and 1893-08-05 serializes to -2338.0. The alternate (date1904=true) anchors at 1904-01-01 which is day 0.0. Time serials add a fractional day portion to the integer day count.

trigger_keywords: password hashing for sheet protection
how is the protection password stored
what hash algorithm is used for the worksheet password
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The password is never stored verbatim. It is converted to a short uppercase hexadecimal hash digest and emitted as the `password` attribute on the sheetProtection element. For example, the password 'fish' produces the hash 'CA3F'. The hashing follows the legacy XOR-based spreadsheet password hash algorithm.

trigger_keywords: type inference rules for string values
what makes a string classify as iso_8601 vs string
when is a datetime string treated as a temporal literal
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A string value is inferred as iso_8601 only when it matches the combined date-and-time format WITHOUT a timezone offset (e.g. '2008-08-30T01:45:36.123' → iso_8601). If the string contains a timezone offset (e.g. '+09:00' or 'Z') it is classified as plain string. Scientific notation strings that are in range (magnitude not exceeding ~1e308) are classified as float; out-of-range (e.g. '1e309') become string.

trigger_keywords: absolute range format with sheet name quoting
how should special characters in sheet names be escaped
XML escaping and quote doubling for sheet names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For absolute references the sheet name is wrapped in single quotes, embedded single quotes are doubled (e.g. ' → ''), and XML-reserved characters are entity-escaped: < → <, > → >, " → ". Row and column are both pinned with $ anchors. Example: sheet name `Sheet <'>" 1` becomes `'Sheet <''>" 1'!$A$1:$B$1`.

trigger_keywords: error output format
what token is returned on invalid input
difference between error=argument and error=invalid_request
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two error tokens exist: `error=argument` is emitted when a value or validation problem occurs (negative margin, non-boolean flag, unrecognized enum, out-of-range coordinate, missing required field like a defined name's name). `error=invalid_request` is emitted when the request itself is structurally unusable. No stack traces, exception names, or runtime messages are ever exposed.

trigger_keywords: column width maximum
what happens when column width exceeds the limit
max allowed column width value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The maximum permitted column width is 255. Any width value exceeding 255 is clamped down to 255. For example, a requested width of 31337 is serialized as width="255". When a width is provided, bestFit="1" and customWidth="1" are also emitted.

trigger_keywords: pane top-left cell derivation
how is topLeftCell inferred when not provided
frozen pane default top left cell
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the pane state is 'frozen' and no topLeftCell is explicitly supplied, it is derived from the split positions: the top-left cell column comes from x_split and the row from y_split, each incremented by one to produce the first visible cell in the bottom-right region. For example x_split=0, y_split=2 yields topLeftCell='A3'. Split positions default to 0.

trigger_keywords: sheet protection default values
which sheet protection flags default to on vs off
default boolean state of sheetProtection attributes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Sheet protection defaults: sheet=1 (on), objects=0 (off), scenarios=0 (off), formatCells=1, formatColumns=1, formatRows=1, insertColumns=1, insertRows=1, insertHyperlinks=1, deleteColumns=1, deleteRows=1, selectLockedCells=0 (off), sort=1, autoFilter=1, pivotTables=1, selectUnlockedCells=0 (off). An empty props object produces all these defaults.

trigger_keywords: page margin defaults
default margin values when not specified
what are the default inch values for page margins
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Default page margins in inches: left=0.75, right=0.75, top=1.0, bottom=1.0, header=0.5, footer=0.5. Any margin that is omitted from the input props uses its default. Any negative margin value must be rejected with error=argument.

trigger_keywords: formula cell serialization
how are formulas stripped of leading equals sign
array formula XML format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A string starting with '=' and escape_formulas=false is treated as a formula: the leading '=' is stripped and the expression goes into a <f> element with t="str" on the <c>. An array formula wrapped in braces like '{=SUM(...)}' is serialized as <f t="array" ref="cellref"> with braces and leading = removed. When escape_formulas=true, the string is emitted verbatim as an inline string with t="inlineStr" and <is><t> wrapping.

trigger_keywords: output trailing newline
should stdout output end with a newline
no trailing newline requirement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The entry point must write the result string to stdout with NO trailing newline and nothing else — no logs, prompts, or metadata. Multiple result items within a single response are joined by a single newline ('\n') character between them.

trigger_keywords: boolean serialization in XML
how are true and false rendered in XML output
boolean flag XML encoding
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Boolean flags in XML fragments are always rendered as '1' for true and '0' for false, never as the words 'true' or 'false'. This applies to printOptions, sheetProtection, pane attributes, filter column flags, and all other boolean XML attributes throughout the engine.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'print_options', 'sheet_protection', 'pane', 'selection', 'cfvo', 'auto_filter_range', 'filter_column', 'defined_name', 'column_label', 'row_label', 'parse_cell_name', 'cell_reference', 'expand_range', 'bounding_range', 'merge', 'infer_type', 'cast', 'serialize_date', 'serialize_time', 'serialize_cell', 'serialize_row', 'serialize_column', 'page_margins'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled it in the auth module a while back, just a short hash
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The legacy XOR-based spreadsheet password hashing algorithm used in the sheetProtection serializer, which produces a short uppercase hex digest (e.g. password='fish' → 'CA3F'), defined in feature11_sheet_protection.json and the sheet protection serialization record

context_pointers.vague_reference: the weird number format spreadsheets use internally for dates
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Spreadsheet date serial numbers: integer day-counts from an epoch (either the default pre-1900 epoch where 1900-01-01=2.0, or the 1904 epoch where 1904-01-01=0.0), with fractional parts representing time-of-day, as specified in feature5_1_date_serial.json and feature5_2_time_serial.json

For absolute range references, sheet names require both XML entity-escaping (< → <, > → >, " → ") AND single-quote doubling (' → ''), as shown in feature3_2_bounding_range.json test case with sheet name 'Sheet <\'>" 1'

context_pointers.vague_reference: clean normalized token for errors, not raw exceptions
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two specific error tokens: `error=argument` for value/validation failures and `error=invalid_request` for structurally malformed requests — no other text, no stack traces, defined in the Execution Contract section of start.md

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details about that constraint documented. Please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature, or refer to the Execution Contract and Core Features sections in start.md for the authoritative specification.

feature10_print_options.json.description: Serialize the worksheet print options to their XML fragment. Four independent boolean flags control whether grid lines are printed, whether row/column headings are printed, and whether the printed content is centered horizontally and vertically. Each flag defaults to off. A flag only accepts a boolean-ish value; a non-boolean value (such as an arbitrary integer) is rejected and reported as a normalized error. The emitted fragment carries all four flags rendered as 1 or 0.

feature11_sheet_protection.json.description: Serialize the per-sheet protection settings to their XML fragment. A set of independent boolean toggles governs what a viewer may do on a protected sheet: whether the sheet itself is protected, whether objects and scenarios are locked, whether formatting of cells, columns and rows is allowed, whether columns and rows may be inserted or deleted, whether hyperlinks may be inserted, whether locked and unlocked cells may be selected, and whether sorting, auto-filtering and pivot tables are permitted. Most permissions default to enabled while sheet protection and object/scenario locking and cell selection default to their off state. An optional clear-text password is accepted and stored only as a short hash digest, never echoed back verbatim. Each toggle accepts only a boolean-ish value; a non-boolean value is rejected and reported as a normalized error. The emitted fragment lists every toggle as 1 or 0, with the password hash appended when present.

cases.expected_output: <sheetProtection sheet="1" objects="0" scenarios="0" formatCells="1" formatColumns="1" formatRows="1" insertColumns="1" insertRows="1" insertHyperlinks="1" deleteColumns="1" deleteRows="1" selectLockedCells="0" sort="1" autoFilter="1" pivotTables="1" selectUnlockedCells="0" />

<sheetProtection sheet="1" objects="1" scenarios="1" formatCells="1" formatColumns="1" formatRows="1" insertColumns="1" insertRows="1" insertHyperlinks="1" deleteColumns="1" deleteRows="1" selectLockedCells="1" sort="1" autoFilter="1" pivotTables="1" selectUnlockedCells="0" password="CA3F" />

feature12_1_pane.json.description: Serialize a worksheet pane (a frozen or split view region) to its XML fragment. The pane records which region is active, the split state (frozen, split, or frozen-and-split), the horizontal and vertical split positions, and the top-left visible cell of the bottom-right region. Region and split-state names are accepted in a snake-case spelling and rendered in the wire fragment in their camel-case spelling. The split positions are non-negative integers and default to zero. When the state is frozen and no top-left cell is supplied, the top-left cell is derived from the split positions. An unrecognized region or split-state name is rejected and reported as a normalized error.

feature12_2_selection.json.description: Serialize a pane selection to its XML fragment. A selection records the active cell, an optional active-cell index within a non-contiguous reference list, the pane region the selection belongs to, and the sequence of selected references. The pane region name is accepted in a snake-case spelling and rendered in its camel-case spelling. The active cell and reference sequence must be textual; the active-cell index must be a non-negative integer; an unrecognized pane region is rejected and reported as a normalized error.

feature13_cfvo.json.description: Serialize a conditional-format threshold value object to its XML fragment. The object describes one interpolation point used by gradient color scales, data bars and icon sets. It carries a type (one of: minimum, maximum, a literal number, a percentage, a percentile, or a formula), a value, and a greater-than-or-equal flag that defaults to enabled and controls whether icon-set thresholds use the inclusive comparison. The type is restricted to the recognized set; an unrecognized type is rejected and reported as a normalized error. The value accepts anything renderable as text.

feature14_1_auto_filter_range.json.description: Compute the workbook-level defined-name formula for an auto-filter range. Given a worksheet name and a rectangular range over that sheet (start cell and end cell separated by a colon), the result is the absolute reference of that range, anchored on both row and column, prefixed with the quoted sheet name. This is the reference a spreadsheet stores so it can re-apply the filter. Inputs supply the sheet name, the seed rows of data, and the range; one reference string is emitted.

feature14_2_filter_column.json.description: Serialize an auto-filter column to its XML fragment. A filter column targets one zero-based column position within the filtered range and holds a discrete-value filter: a list of literal values; only rows whose cell in that column matches one of the listed values remain visible. Two boolean flags control whether the filter drop-down button is shown and whether it is hidden. The emitted fragment carries the column position and any non-default flags, and nests one filter entry per listed value. The column position must be an integer and the filter kind must be a recognized kind; an invalid position or filter kind is rejected and reported as a normalized error.

feature15_defined_name.json.description: Serialize a workbook-level defined name to its XML fragment. A defined name binds a label to a formula or range reference. Serialization requires a name; attempting to serialize without one is rejected and reported as a normalized error. An optional hidden flag is emitted when enabled. The name is written verbatim (never case-folded) and the formula reference becomes the element's text body. Inputs supply the formula and the name plus optional flags.

feature1_1_column_label.json.description: Convert a zero-based column ordinal into its spreadsheet column label using the bijective base-26 scheme (the first column is a single letter, and after the final single-letter label the labels grow to two letters, then three, and so on). Given a list of column ordinals, emit one label per line in the same order.

cases.expected_output:
A
Z
B
AA
AAA
AAZ
ABA
BZU

feature1_2_row_label.json.description: Render a standalone one-based row label from a zero-based row ordinal. Row labels are simply the ordinal plus one. Given a list of row ordinals, emit one label per line in the same order.

feature2_1_parse_cell_name.json.description: Parse a textual cell name composed of a column label followed by a one-based row number into a pair of zero-based indices (column ordinal and row ordinal). Given a list of cell names, emit one comma-separated index pair per line, column first then row.

feature2_2_cell_reference.json.description: Compose a full cell reference (column label plus one-based row number) from a zero-based column ordinal and a zero-based row ordinal. Each input pair is rendered as one reference, emitted one per line in order.

feature3_1_expand_range.json.description: Expand a rectangular reference range (a start cell and an end cell separated by a colon) into the full grid of individual cell references it covers, ordered row by row and, within each row, left to right. Each requested range is rendered as lines of comma-separated references, and successive ranges are separated by a line containing two equals signs.

feature3_2_bounding_range.json.description: Compute the bounding reference range that spans a collection of cells, independent of the order in which the cells are supplied. A relative result is just the top-left and bottom-right references joined by a colon. An absolute result additionally pins both row and column with anchor markers and prefixes the originating sheet name, with the sheet name quoted and any embedded quote characters doubled; reserved XML characters in the sheet name are entity-escaped. Cells are laid out left to right in a single row; the 'pick' list selects cells by position, or the whole row is used when 'pick' is omitted.

feature3_3_merged_range.json.description: Compute the merged reference range produced by merging one cell with a target, where the target is given either as a textual cell reference or as another cell in the same row. The resulting range runs from the top-left to the bottom-right of the two endpoints and is independent of which endpoint initiates the merge. Cells are placed left to right in a single row and referenced by position; one merged range string is emitted.

feature4_1_type_inference.json.description: Infer the storage data type of a value when no explicit type is declared. Calendar dates, clock times, and boolean values are recognized from their native object kinds. Remaining values are classified by textual shape: a bare optionally-signed run of digits is an integer; a decimal or scientific-notation number whose magnitude is within the representable floating range is a float; a date-time string in the extended combined-calendar-and-time notation without a zone offset is treated as a temporal literal; a styled-text object is rich text; everything else, including out-of-range scientific notation and offset-bearing date-time strings, is a string. Each input value is tagged with its source kind, and one inferred type name is emitted per value.

cases.expected_output:
float
float
float
string
float
integer
date
time
string
string
string
integer
boolean
boolean
richtext
string
iso_8601

feature4_2_value_casting.json.description: Coerce a value into a declared storage type and report the resulting stored value. Casting to the textual type yields the value's string form; casting to the integer or float type yields its numeric form; casting a boolean yields one for true and zero for false; a null value stays null regardless of declared type; and casting to the zone-offset date-time literal type leaves the supplied text unchanged. Each item pairs a target type with a tagged source value, and one stored value is emitted per item (a null value renders as an empty line).

feature5_1_date_serial.json.description: Convert a calendar date to its spreadsheet serial number, the count of days from the workbook's epoch. Two epoch conventions are exercised: the default convention whose day-zero predates the start of 1900, and the alternate convention anchored at the start of 1904. Each case selects an epoch convention and lists dates; one serial number is emitted per date.

feature5_2_time_serial.json.description: Convert an instant in time to its fractional spreadsheet serial number, where the integer part is days from the workbook epoch and the fractional part is the elapsed fraction of the day. Two epoch conventions are exercised: the default convention whose day-zero predates the start of 1900, and the alternate convention anchored at the start of 1904. Times are supplied in the zero-offset zone. Each case selects an epoch convention and lists instants; one serial number is emitted per instant.

feature6_cell_serialization.json.description: Serialize a single grid cell to its XML fragment. The opening element always carries the cell reference (column label plus one-based row) and a style index. A null-valued cell is written as a self-closing element with no body. A numeric cell carries a numeric type marker and a value body. A formula cell (a string beginning with an equals sign, when formula escaping is disabled) is written with a string type marker and a formula body holding the expression without its leading equals sign. An array-formula cell (wrapped in braces with a leading equals sign) is written with a formula body marked as an array over its own reference. When formula escaping is enabled, or for an explicitly textual value, the content is emitted verbatim as an inline string. Inputs describe one cell plus its row and column position; the emitted fragment is the exact XML string.

feature7_row_serialization.json.description: Serialize a worksheet row to its XML fragment. The row element always carries its one-based row position. Optional row-level attributes are emitted when set: a hidden flag, an outline nesting level, a collapsed flag, a custom-format flag together with a row style index, and, when a height is supplied, a custom-height flag together with the height value. Any cells belonging to the row are serialized in order inside the row element. Inputs describe the row's values, optional attributes, and its row position; the emitted fragment is the exact XML string.

cases.expected_output: <row hidden="1" outlineLevel="2" collapsed="1" customFormat="1" s="1" customHeight="1" ht="20" r="1" ></row>
<row customHeight="1" ht="20" r="1" ><c r="A1" s="0" t="n"><v>1</v></c></row>

feature8_column_serialization.json.description: Serialize a column-information record to its XML fragment. The element carries the first and last affected column indices. When a width is assigned, the record also records the width together with a best-fit flag and a custom-width flag; any width exceeding the maximum permitted column width is clamped down to that maximum. Inputs describe the column span and an optional width; the emitted fragment is the exact XML string.

feature9_page_margins.json.description: Serialize the printable page-margin settings to their XML fragment. Margins are given in inches for the left, right, top, bottom, header and footer edges. Any margins not supplied keep their defaults (three-quarters of an inch for left/right, one inch for top/bottom, half an inch for header/footer). Every margin must be a non-negative number; supplying a negative margin is rejected and reported as a normalized error. The emitted fragment lists all six margins.

cases.expected_output: <pageMargins left="1.1" right="1.2" top="1.3" bottom="1.4" header="0.8" footer="0.9" />
<pageMargins left="0.75" right="0.75" top="1.0" bottom="1.0" header="0.5" footer="0.5" />
